text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Division of GI Oncology

Patient: Raymond Kowalski
Age: 59 years
Attending: [Medical Oncologist, GI Oncology]
Visit Type: Established patient follow-up — molecular profiling integration and disease management

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Mr. Kowalski presents today for integration of repeat genomic profiling results from his recent right lower lobe pulmonary nodule biopsy with his ongoing clinical status. He is approximately 18-19 months from his initial stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma diagnosis. He has been on FOLFIRI plus ramu as second-line therapy and is currently in his seventh month of this regimen. We are reviewing the FoundationOne CDx results today, discussing implications for ongoing management, and addressing goals of care.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old retired steel mill worker from Pittsburgh with a well-established history of KRAS G12D-mutant, pMMR/MSS stage IV (now M1b) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma. His disease course is reviewed briefly:

He was diagnosed approximately 18-19 months ago with synchronous liver metastases (four bilobar lesions, largest 3.2 cm). Initial molecular profiling from the sigmoid primary demonstrated KRAS G12D (dominant driver), TP53 R248W, APC E1554*, SMAD4 R361H, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion — a fairly adverse genomic profile. He completed 12 cycles of mFOLFOX6 plus bev achieving a partial response (CEA down from 148 to 22.8, significant hepatic downsizing), followed by transition to maintenance LV5FU2 plus Avastin per an OPTIMOX-type strategy. This was complicated by Grade 2 Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy necessitating dose reduction and ultimately Eloxatin discontinuation.

Approximately 10 months into his course, surveillance CT demonstrated disease progression with three new bilateral pulmonary nodules (up to 0.8 cm RLL) and hepatic growth of the Segment VI and IVb lesions, accompanied by CEA re-elevation to 67.3. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramu. Initial response was favorable — the hepatic lesions and pulmonary nodules demonstrated partial response, with CEA declining to 44.1 at the 4-month mark. More recently (7 months into second-line therapy, 17 months from diagnosis), his most recent CT showed borderline stable disease with mild creep in multiple lesions, and CEA ticked up to 51.8. CA 19-9 at that time was 162.

Given the trajectory — best PR followed by slow loss of control — we proceeded with a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the RLL pulmonary nodule approximately one month ago to confirm the diagnosis (met CRC confirmed: CK20+, CDX2+, CK7-, TTF-1-) and to obtain fresh tissue for repeat molecular profiling, specifically to look for acquired resistance mechanisms.

The repeat FoundationOne CDx report has now returned and is the primary focus of today's visit.

MOLECULAR PROFILING INTEGRATION:
The repeat profile from the pulmonary met shows several important findings relative to baseline:

— RETAINED (clonal backbone preserved): KRAS G12D (AF 47%), TP53 R248W (AF 51%), APC E1554* (AF 48%), SMAD4 R361H (AF 41%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, FBXW7 R505C (minor subclonal drift). This confirms the RLL nodule is indeed derived from the original tumor clone and is not an independent primary.

— NEWLY DETECTED / ACQUIRED: (1) KRAS G12V subclone (AF 11%) — a second KRAS mutation at the same codon 12, likely representing intratumoral heterogeneity or a newly emergent resistance subclone; (2) PIK3CA E545K (AF 18%) — acquired helical domain activating mutation in PI3K alpha, not present at baseline, activating a parallel survival pathway; (3) MYC amplification (CN 8.2, up from ~3 at baseline) — significant focal amplification at 8q24 superimposed on the prior 8q gain; (4) RB1 p.Q494* (AF 14%) — acquired truncating mutation providing cell cycle checkpoint bypass, complementing the pre-existing CDKN2A deletion.

— MSS phenotype retained, TMB 6 mut/Mb (low). The tumor has not hypermutated. No HER2 amplification, no NTRK fusions, no actionable targets.

Summary: The molecular evolution is consistent with clonal selection under chemotherapy pressure. The acquisition of PIK3CA E545K and MYC amplification in particular suggests progressive genomic instability and activation of alternative survival pathways. There is no newly emerging immunotherapy target and no approved targeted agent that directly addresses this profile in the colorectal cancer context.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Constitutional: Moderate fatigue, increased recently compared to 3 months ago. No fevers or chills. Weight stable (he reports he has not lost weight over the past month, BMI ~27).
Gastrointestinal: Grade 1 diarrhea — 2-3 loose stools per day, managed adequately with occasional loperamide. No hematochezia, melena, or abdominal pain. Appetite mildly decreased. No nausea or vomiting at present.
Neurological: Residual Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, bilateral fingertips and toes — tingling, stable, not worsening. No new neurologic complaints.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea on exertion. No lower extremity edema. Blood pressure managed on dual antihypertensive therapy.
Respiratory: No new cough, hemoptysis, or dyspnea. He is aware of the pulmonary nodules but reports no pulmonary symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: Some mild generalized stiffness and deconditioning. He has reduced his walking activity over the past month due to fatigue.
Psychosocial: He acknowledges feeling more anxious since the biopsy and the uncertainty around what the new genomic results mean for his prognosis. He has begun meeting with the social worker and found it helpful. Wife is present at today's visit and is very engaged.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 141/88 mmHg (left arm); Heart rate 74 bpm; Respiratory rate 16/min; SpO2 97% on room air; Weight 81 kg; ECOG Performance Status: 1.
General: Alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Mildly tired-appearing. Well-nourished. Accompanied by wife.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, nontender. No guarding or rigidity. Liver edge palpable approximately 1-2 cm below the right costal margin — slightly more prominent than prior visit, consistent with known hepatic metastases. No splenomegaly. Normal bowel sounds. Port-a-cath site: right chest wall, no erythema or tenderness.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Mild reduction in vibratory sensation at bilateral great toes (stable, consistent with known Eloxatin neuropathy). Coordination intact. Gait steady.
Skin/Extremities: Grade 1 alopecia, consistent with iri. Mild dry skin bilaterally on hands. No significant hand-foot syndrome.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY)
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CBC: WBC 4.8 K/uL, Hgb 9.8 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, slight worsening from 10.0; no transfusion indicated), Hct 29.6%, MCV 87 fL, Platelets 162 K/uL, ANC 1.7 K/uL (Grade 1 neutropenia, borderline).
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138, Potassium 3.9, Bicarbonate 24, BUN 16, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60 — adequate renal function for current regimen). Glucose 112 (mild, diet-controlled DM2). Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL; Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL; AST 74 U/L (elevated, up from 62 at prior visit); ALT 81 U/L (elevated, up from 68). Alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L (mildly elevated; up from 118 at prior visit) — trend consistent with mild hepatic progression. Total protein 6.8, Albumin 3.6 g/dL (low-normal; mild nutritional concern).
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1 (normal).
Tumor Markers: CEA: 58.4 ng/mL (up from 51.8 at last visit, now 2.5 months ago — continued slow uptrend). CA 19-9: 178 U/mL (up from 162).
Urine Protein:Creatinine Ratio: 0.26 (acceptable; monitoring for Cyramza-associated proteinuria).
LFTs trend: Progressive upward drift in transaminases and AP over the past two visits, consistent with hepatic disease activity.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMAGING REVIEW
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (performed approximately 6 weeks ago): Stable disease overall — Segment VI liver lesion 1.9 x 1.7 cm (minimal increase), Segment IVb 0.9 x 0.8 cm, Segment II 0.6 x 0.5 cm; pulmonary nodules stable. No new sites of disease. Formally interpreted as stable disease, though there has been a slow, consistent upward drift in most lesions since the nadir achieved approximately 3 months into second-line therapy. Clinical trajectory with rising tumor markers, increasing LFTs, and creeping lesion sizes raises concern for early loss of disease control, though no single criterion has clearly crossed into progression territory.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ASSESSMENT AND PLAN
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
IMPRESSION:
Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with metastatic KRAS G12D-mutant, pMMR/MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (stage IV, M1b — liver and lung metastases), now approximately 18-19 months from diagnosis. He has received two lines of chemotherapy. Repeat molecular profiling from a pulmonary metastasis demonstrates meaningful clonal evolution: PIK3CA E545K acquisition, high-level MYC amplification, subclonal RB1 loss, and a secondary KRAS G12V subclone — all consistent with genomic evolution under chemotherapy-driven selection pressure. The MSS phenotype and low TMB are unchanged, definitively excluding immunotherapy. No newly actionable FDA-approved target has emerged in the colorectal cancer context.

Clinically, he has been on FOLFIRI plus Cyramza for approximately 7 months. He achieved a partial response early in this regimen, which has since narrowed to borderline stable disease. His tumor markers are slowly rising, his liver enzymes are drifting upward, and his fatigue has increased. His performance status remains ECOG 1 but is trending in the wrong direction. This constellation — molecular evolution, rising markers, rising LFTs, creeping disease without unequivocal CT-based progression — suggests he is approaching early loss of disease control on current second-line therapy.

ACTION ITEMS:

1. CONTINUE FOLFIRI PLUS ramu FOR NOW, with repeat CT imaging in 6 weeks:
   — Current CT shows formal stable disease; no clear indication to change therapy today.
   — However, I am concerned about the trajectory. We will image sooner (6 weeks vs. 8 weeks) given the marker trend. If next CT demonstrates any unambiguous hepatic or pulmonary progression, we will transition to third-line therapy without delay.
   — Discussed with Mr. Kowalski and his wife: We are watching carefully, and if the disease is clearly growing on the next scan, we will talk about what comes next. He understands and agrees with this plan.

2. THIRD-LINE PLANNING — discussed with patient today as contingency:
   — If/when FOLFIRI/Cyramza fails, standard third-line options for MSS, KRAS-mutant mCRC include:
     a) rego (Stivarga, 160 mg daily days 1-21 q28 days) — CORRECT trial demonstrated modest OS benefit (HR 0.77); starting at 80 mg/day with planned escalation to 120 then 160 mg as tolerated (ReDOS approach) preferred given tolerability.
     b) TAS-102/trifluridine-tipiracil (Lonsurf, 35 mg/m2 BID days 1-5 and 8-12 q28 days) — RECOURSE trial; modest but real OS benefit in refractory mCRC.
     c) TAS-102 plus bev: SUNLIGHT trial demonstrated improved OS with this combination vs. TAS-102 monotherapy (HR 0.61) in third-line or beyond; this is my preferred third-line approach if Avastin has been adequately washed out from ramu.
     — Importantly: the newly detected KRAS G12D mutation remains the dominant driver. I will be looking into available KRAS G12D inhibitor trials (MRTX1133, adagrasib-based combinations) — this is an area of active development and he may be a candidate. I will contact our clinical trials office today.

3. CLINICAL TRIAL REFERRAL:
   — Patient has previously expressed interest in clinical trials when he progresses. Referral placed to clinical trials office to evaluate current availability of:
     a) KRAS G12D-directed therapy trials (selective KRAS G12D inhibitors or combinations)
     b) Any basket trials for PIK3CA-mutant solid tumors (he now has PIK3CA E545K)
     c) RAS/MAPK pathway co-inhibition strategies
   — Results pending; will contact patient when options are identified.

4. HYPERTENSION:
   — BP today 141/88 mmHg, borderline elevated. On lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily.
   — Will increase amlodipine to 10 mg daily. Recheck BP at next infusion visit.

5. ANEMIA:
   — Hgb 9.8, down from 10.0. Grade 1. Fatigue likely multifactorial (anemia, cumulative chemotherapy, disease burden).
   — No transfusion indicated at this time. Iron studies ordered to rule out iron deficiency component (ferritin, iron, TIBC). If iron deficient, will add IV iron supplementation.
   — ESA (erythropoiesis-stimulating agent) not recommended in this palliative, non-curative-intent setting per ASCO guidelines.

6. NUTRITION AND SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   — Albumin 3.6, mild decline. Referred to clinical dietitian for nutritional counseling.
   — Physical therapy consult placed — deconditioning, balance exercises (residual neuropathy).
   — Palliative care is now following — will continue to coordinate for symptom management, goals of care, and advance care planning. Patient has completed advance directive, wife designated as healthcare proxy.

7. GOALS OF CARE:
   — Reaffirmed today. Patient understands his cancer is incurable. Primary goals remain: extending meaningful survival while maintaining quality of life and independence. He wants to be able to spend time with his grandchildren and maintain some degree of functional independence.
   — I reassured him that there are meaningful options remaining. We talked honestly about the trajectory — that the disease is evolving and that we are likely approaching a need to change therapy, and that the window for clinical trial enrollment (especially KRAS G12D trials) would be best captured sooner rather than later.
   — Patient and wife expressed gratitude for the candor. They are realistic and coping as well as can be expected.

RETURN VISIT: Patient will return in 2 weeks for next FOLFIRI/ramu infusion (Cycle 9 Day 1) and sooner imaging planning. Repeat CT scheduled in 6 weeks.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes, more than half of which was devoted to counseling and care coordination.

[Electronically signed — Medical Oncology, GI Oncology Division]
